## **Data driven semi-supervised learning**

**Maria-Florina Balcan** School of Computer Science Carnegie Mellon University Pittsburgh, PA 15213 `ninamf@cs.cmu.edu`

## **Dravyansh Sharma**

Department of Computer Science Carnegie Mellon University Pittsburgh, PA 15213 `dravyans@cs.cmu.edu`

## **Abstract**

We consider a novel data driven approach for designing semi-supervised learning algorithms that can effectively learn with only a small number of labeled examples. We focus on graph-based techniques, where the unlabeled examples are connected in a graph under the implicit assumption that similar nodes likely have similar labels. Over the past two decades, several elegant graph-based semi-supervised learning algorithms for inferring the labels of the unlabeled examples given the graph and a few labeled examples have been proposed. However, the problem of how to create the graph (which impacts the practical usefulness of these methods significantly) has been relegated to heuristics and domain-specific art, and no general principles have been proposed. In this work we present a novel data driven approach for learning the graph and provide strong formal guarantees in both the distributional and online learning formalizations. We show how to leverage problem instances coming from an underlying problem domain to learn the graph hyperparameters for commonly used parametric families of graphs that provably perform well on new instances from the same domain. We obtain low regret and efficient algorithms in the online setting, and generalization guarantees in the distributional setting. We also show how to combine several very different similarity metrics and learn multiple hyperparameters, our results hold for large classes of problems. We expect some of the tools and techniques we develop along the way to be of independent interest, for data driven algorithms more generally.

## **1 Introduction**

In recent years machine learning has found gainful application in diverse domains. A major bottleneck of the currently used approaches is the heavy dependence on expensive labeled data. Advances in cheap computing and storage have made it relatively easier to store and process large amounts of unlabeled data. Therefore, an important focus of the present research community is to develop general domain-independent methods to learn effectively from the unlabeled data, along with a small amount of labels. Achieving this goal would significantly elevate the state-of-the-art machine intelligence, which currently lags behind the human capability of learning from a few labeled examples. Our work is a step in this direction, and provides algorithms and guarantees that enable fundamental techniques for semi-supervised learning to provably adapt to problem domains.

Graph-based approaches have been popular for learning from unlabeled data for the past two decades [Zhu and Goldberg, 2009]. Labeled and unlabeled examples form the graph nodes and (possibly weighted) edges denote the feature similarity between examples. The graph therefore captures how each example is related to other examples, and by optimizing a suitably regularized objective over it one obtains an efficient discriminative, nonparametric method for learning the labels. There are several well-studied ways to define and regularize an objective on the graph [Chapelle et al., 2010],

**Problem formulation** Given sets _L_ and _U_ of labeled and unlabeled examples respectively, and a similarity metric _d_ over the data, the goal is to use _d_ to extrapolate labels in _L_ to _U_ . A graph _G_ is constructed with _L_ + _U_ as the nodes and weighted edges _W_ with _w_ ( _u, v_ ) = _g_ ( _d_ ( _u, v_ )) for some _g_ : R _≥_ 0 _→_ R _≥_ 0. We seek labels _f_ ( _·_ ) for nodes _u_ of _G_ which minimize a regularized loss function _l_ ( _f_ ) = _α_[�] _v∈L_[ˆ] _[l]_[(] _[f]_[(] _[v]_[)] _[, y][v]_[) +] _[ βH]_[(] _[f, W]_[) +] _[ γ][ ∥][f][∥]_[2][,][under][some][constraints][on] _[f]_[.][The][objective] ˆ _Hl_ ( _f_ captures the( _v_ ) _, yv_ ) is the misclassification loss (computed here on labeled examples). _smoothness_ (regularization) induced by the graph (see Table 1 for examples) and

The graph _G_ takes a central position in this formulation. However, the majority of the research effort on this problem has focused on how to design and optimize the regularized loss function _l_ ( _f_ ), the effectiveness of which crucially depends on _G_ . There is no known principled study on how to build _G_ and prior work largely treats this as a domain-specific art [Chapelle et al., 2010]. Is it possible to acquire the required domain expertise, without involving human experts? In this work we provide an affirmative answer by formulating graph selection as _data-driven design._ More precisely, we are required to solve not only one instance, but multiple instances of the underlying algorithmic problem that come from the same domain [Gupta and Roughgarden, 2016, Balcan, 2020]. We show learning a near-optimal graph over commonly used infinite parameterized families is possible in both online and distributional settings. In the process we generalize and extend data-driven learning techniques, and obtain practical methods to build the graphs with strong guarantees. In particular, we show how the techniques can learn several parameters at once, and also learn a broader class of parameters than previously known.
**Our contributions and key challenges.** We present a first theoretically grounded work for graphbased learning from limited labeled data, while extending general data-driven design techniques.

_Data-driven algorithm design._ Firstly, for one dimensional loss functions, we show a novel structural result which applies when discontinuities (for loss as function of the algorithm parameter) occur along roots of exponential polynomials with random coefficients with bounded joint distributions (previously known only for algebraic polynomials in Balcan et al. [2020b]). This is crucial for showing learnability in the Gaussian graph kernels setting. Secondly, Balcan et al. [2020b] only applies when the discontinuities occur along algebraic curves with random coefficients in just two dimensions. By a novel algebraic and learning theoretic argument we are able to analyze higher (arbitrary constant number of) dimensions, making the technique much more generally applicable.

_Semi-supervised learning._ We examine commonly used parameterized graph families, denoted by general notation _G_ ( _ρ_ ), where _ρ_ corresponds to a semi-supervised learning algorithm. We consider online and distributional settings, providing efficient algorithms to obtain low regret and low error respectively for learning _ρ_ . Most previously studied settings involve polynomially many discontinuities for loss as function of the hyperparameter _ρ_ on a fixed instance, implying efficient algorithms, which may not be the case for our setting. To resolve this, we describe efficient semi-bandit implementations, and in particular introduce a novel min-cut and flow recomputation algorithm on graphs with continuously changing edge weights which may be of independent interest. For the distributional setting, we provide asymptotically tight bounds on the pseudodimension of the parameter learning problem. Our lower bounds expose worst case challenges, and involve precise constructions of problem instances by setting node similarities which make assigning labels provably hard.

2

Our techniques are extremely general and are shown to apply for nearly all combinations of optimization algorithms (Table 1) and parametric graph families (Definition 1).

**Related work** _Semi-supervised learning_ is a paradigm for learning from labeled and unlabeled data (Zhu and Goldberg [2009]). It resembles human learning behavior more closely than fully supervised and fully unsupervised models (Zhu et al. [2007], Gibson et al. [2013]). A popular approach for semi-supervised learning is to optimize a graph-based objective. Several methods have been proposed to predict labels given a graph including _st_ -mincuts (Blum and Chawla [2001]), soft mincuts that optimize a harmonic objective (Zhu et al. [2003]), label propagation (Xiaojin and Zoubin [2002]), and many more (Shi and Malik [2000], Belkin et al. [2006]). All algorithms have comparable performance provided the graph _G_ encodes the problem well [Zhu and Goldberg, 2009]. However, it is not clear how to create the graph itself on which the extensive literature stands, barring some heuristics (Zhu et al. [2005], Zemel and Carreira-Perpiñán [2004]). Sindhwani et al. [2005] construct _warped_ kernels aligned with the data geometry, but the performance may vary strongly with warping and it is not clear how to optimize over it. We provide the first techniques that yield provably near-optimal graphs.

Gupta and Roughgarden [2016, 2017] define a formal learning framework for selecting algorithms from a family of heuristics or setting hyperparameters. It is further developed by Balcan et al. [2017] and noted as a fundamental algorithm design perspective [Blum, 2020]. It has been successfully applied to several combinatorial problems like integer programming and clustering [Balcan et al., 2018a, 2019, 2018c] and for giving powerful guarantees like adversarial robustness, adaptive learning and differential privacy [Balcan et al., 2018b, 2020a,c, Vitercik et al., 2019, Balcan et al., 2020e,d]. Balcan et al. [2018b, 2020b] introduce general data-driven design techniques under some smoothness assumptions. We extend the techniques to significantly broader problem settings, and investigate the structure of graph-based label learning formulation to apply the new techniques.

## **3 New general dispersion-based tools for data-driven design**

We present new general tools for analyzing data-driven algorithms. Our new tools apply to a very broad class of algorithm design problems, for which we derive sufficient _smoothness_ conditions to infer dispersion of a random sequence of problems, i.e. the algorithmic performance as a function of the algorithm parameters is dispersed. Recall that dispersion, roughly speaking, captures the rate at which discontinuities concentrate in any region of the domain. Balcan et al. [2020b] provide a general tool for verifying dispersion if non-Lipschitzness occurs along roots of (algebraic) polynomials in one and two dimensions. We improve upon their results in two major ways.

Our first result is that dispersion for one-dimensional loss functions follows when the points of discontinuity occur at the roots of exponential polynomials if the coefficients are random, lie within a finite range, and are drawn according to a bounded joint distribution. The key idea is use algebraic arguments and Taylor series approximation to show that for any small interval containing roots of the random exponential polynomial, the corresponding sets of coefficients lie on _n −_ 1 dimensional linear subspaces with a probability measure proportional to the length of the interval (Appendix C.3).

## **4 Learning the graph online**

We will warm up this section with a simple example demonstrating the need for and challenges posed by the problem of learning how to build a good graph from data. We consider the setting of learning thresholds for unweighted graphs (Definition 1a). We give a simple demonstration that in a single instance _any threshold_ may be optimal for labelings consistent with graph smoothness assumptions, therefore providing motivation for the learning in our setting. Our construction (depicted in Figure 1) captures the intuition that any unlabeled point may get weakly connected to examples from one class for a small threshold but may get strongly connected to another class as the threshold is increased to a larger value. Therefore depending on the unknown true label either threshold may be optimal or suboptimal, and it makes sense to learn the correct value through repeated problem instances.

## **4.1 Dispersion and online learning**

We consider the problem of learning the graph online. In this setting, we are presented with instances of the problem online and want to learn the best value of the parameter _ρ_ while making predictions. ## **4.1.3 Semi-bandit setting and efficient algorithms.**

Online learning with full information is usually inefficient in practice since it involves computing and working with the entire domain of hyperparameters. For our setting in particular this is computationally infeasible for weighted graphs since the number of pieces (in loss as a piecewise constant function of the parameter) may be exponential in the worst case (see Section 5). Fortunately we have a workaround provided by Balcan et al. [2020b] where dispersion implies learning in a semi-bandit setting as well. This setting differs from the full information online problem as follows. In each round as we select the parameter _ρi_ , we only observe losses for a single interval containing _ρi_ (as opposed to the entire domain). We call the set of these observable intervals the _feedback set_ , and these provide a partition of the domain.

## **5 Distributional setting**

In the distributional setting, we are presented with instances of the problem assumed to be drawn from an unknown distribution _D_ and want to learn the best value of the graph parameter _ρ_ , that is one that minimizes loss _lA_ ( _G_ ( _ρ_ ) _,L,U_ ), in expectation over the data distribution _D_ . We show a divergence in the weighted and unweighted graph learning problems. We analyze and provide asymptotically tight bounds for the pseudodimension of the set of loss functions parameterized by the graph family parameter _ρ_ , i.e. _Hρ_ = _{lA_ ( _G_ ( _ρ_ ) _,L,U_ ) _| ρ ∈P}_ . For learning the unweighted threshold graphs, the pseudodimension is _O_ (log _n_ ) which implies existence of an efficient algorithm with generalization guarantees in this setting. However, the pseudodimension is shown to be Ω( _n_ ) for the weighted graph setting, and therefore smoothness assumptions are necessary for learning over the algorithm family. Both these bounds are shown to be tight up to constant factors.

We also establish uniform convergence guarantees. For the unweighted graph setting, our pseudodimension bounds are sufficient for uniform convergence. We resort to bounding the Rademacher complexity in the weighted graph setting which allows us to prove distribution dependent generalization guarantees, that hold under distributional niceness assumptions of Section 4.1 (unlike pseudodimension which gives generalization guarantees that are worst-case over the distribution). The online learning results above only work for smoothed but adversarial instances, while the pseudodimension-based distributional learning sample complexity results work for any type (no smoothness needed) of independent and identically distributed instances. So these results are not superseded by the online learning results and provide new upper and lower bounds for the problem.

We also show an asymptotically tight lower bound on the pseudodimension of _Hr_ , by presenting a collection of graph thresholds and precisely designed labeling instances which are shattered by the thresholds. ## **6 Experiments**

In this section we evaluate the performance of our learning procedures when finding applicationspecific semi-supervised learning algorithms (i.e. graph parameters). Our experiments[3] demonstrate that the best parameter for different applications varies greatly, and that the techniques presented in this paper can lead to large gains. We look at image classification based on standard pixel embedding.

_Setup_ : We consider the task of semi-supervised classfication on image datasets. We restrict our attention to binary classification and pick two classes (labels 0 or 1) for each dataset. We then draw random subsets of the dataset (with class restriction) of size _n_ = 100 and randomly select _L_ examples for labeling. For any data subset _S_ , we measure distance between any pairs of images using the _L_ 2 distance between their pixel intensities. We would like to determine data-specific good values for _σ_ , when predictions are made by optimizing the harmonic objective (Table 1). We use three popular benchmark datasets — MNIST [LeCun et al., 1998], Omniglot [Lake et al., 2015] and CIFAR-10 [Szegedy et al., 2015]. We generate a random semi-supervised learning instance from the data by sampling 100 random examples and further sampling _L_ random examples from the subset for labeling. _L_ = 10 for MNIST, while _L_ = 20 for Omniglot and CIFAR-10.

_Results and discussion_ : For the MNIST dataset we get optimal parameters with near-perfect classification even with small values of _L_ , while for other datasets the error of the optimal parameter is over 0 _._ 1 even with larger values of _L_ , indicating differences in the inherent difficulties of the classification tasks (like label noise and how well separated the classes are). We examine the full variation of performance of graph-based semi-supervised learning for all possible graphs _G_ ( _σ_ ) for _σ ∈_ [0 _,_ 10]. The losses are piecewise constant and can have large discontinuities in some cases. The optimal parameter values vary with the dataset, but we observe at least 10%, and up to 80%, absolute gaps in performance between optimal and suboptimal values within the same dataset.

Another interesting observation is the variation of optima across data subsets, indicating transductively optimal parameters may not generalize well. We plot the variation of loss with parameter _σ_ for several subsets of the same size _N_ = 100 for MNIST and Omniglot datasets in Figure 2. In MNIST we have two optimal ranges in most subsets but only one shared optimum (around _σ_ = 2) across different subsets. This indicates that local search based techniques that estimate the optimal parameter values on a given data instance may lead to very poor performance on unseen instances. The CIFAR-10 example further shows that the optimal algorithm may not be easy to empirically discern.

We also implement our online algorithms and compute the average regret for finding the optimal graph parameter _σ_ for the different datasets. To obtain smooth curves we plot the average over 50 iterations for learning from 50 problem instances each ( _T_ = 50, Figure 3). We observe fast convergence to the optimal parameter regret for all the datasets considered. The starting part of these curves ( _T_ = 0) indicates regret for randomly setting the graph parameters, averaged over iterations, which is strongly outperformed by our learning algorithms as they learn from problem instances.

## **7 Ethics and broader impact**

This work takes a step in making semi-supervised learning techniques domain independent and more practically effective. The resulting automation reduces dependence on human labelers and domain experts needed in current approaches. Dataset bias and ethics of applications will need to be individually considered when applying our approach to real world problems.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper addresses an important and underexplored problem: the principled, data-driven construction of graphs for graph-based semi-supervised learning (SSL), which has historically been treated as domain-specific art. The contribution is well-motivated and sits at a productive intersection of SSL and the growing literature on data-driven algorithm design, with claimed advances in both areas (handling exponential-polynomial discontinuities and extending dispersion-style analyses beyond two dimensions). The framing is clear, the problem formulation is precise, and Table 1 usefully situates the work within standard SSL objectives. However, several concerns arise even from the introduction. First, the writing is dense and assumes substantial familiarity with the Balcan et al. data-driven design framework; readers outside this niche may struggle without a more intuitive overview of what "learning the graph" concretely means and why prior techniques fail. Second, the contributions are described at a highly technical level with little concrete intuition or illustrative example—e.g., what a learned hyperparameter looks like, or what regret/sample complexity bounds are actually achieved. Third, the practical significance is asserted rather than demonstrated at this stage: given that the central claim is that graph construction "impacts practical usefulness significantly," the introduction would benefit from forward-pointing empirical evidence or at least a concrete motivating scenario. Finally, some technical phrasing (e.g., "exponential polynomials with random coefficients with bounded joint distributions") is introduced without the setup needed to parse it, and typographical issues in the problem formulation block (mangled equations around the loss function) reduce clarity. Overall, the paper appears to make genuine theoretical contributions to an important problem, but accessibility, motivation of the technical novelty, and connection to practice could be substantially strengthened.

comments.title: Table 1 constraints for Normalized cut appear inconsistent with caption definition of L
comments.explanation: The Normalized cut row has constraint f ∈ [0,1]^n which conflicts with the standard normalized cut formulation, and more importantly, standard normalized cut uses the normalized Laplacian L (which is defined in the table caption as D^{-1/2}(D-W)D^{-1/2}) rather than f^T(D-W)f. The Normalized cut row uses f^T(D-W)f like mincut/harmonic, which contradicts the very definition of L given in the caption. Also the constraint f^T f = n^2 combined with f ∈ [0,1]^n is only satisfiable when f is the all-ones vector, making the constraint set essentially empty or trivial.

A reader trying to apply the definition precisely would need to know what C is. Since the definition is imported from Balcan et al. [2020b] and the paper says 'well-known definitions from prior work (Appendix A),' this is likely clarified in the appendix, so this is a minor issue.

comments.title: Theorem 2 excludes n=1 case but φ could have no x-dependence
comments.quote: For _n_ = 1 there are no roots, so assume _n >_ 1.
comments.explanation: I initially wondered whether this is correct. For n=1, φ(x) = a_1 e^{b_1 x}. This has no zeros (assuming a_1 ≠ 0, which has probability 1 under a bounded density), so the claim is correct. However, if b_1 = 0, then φ(x) = a_1 is a constant, and again no zeros (a.s.). So the statement holds. Not an issue.

comments.title: Orthogonality characterization of roots is incorrect for exponential polynomials
comments.quote: Suppose _ρ_ is a root of _φ_ ( _x_ ). Then **a** = ( _a_ 1 _, . . . , an_ ) is orthogonal to _ϱ_ ( _ρ_ ) = ( _e[b]_[1] _[ρ] , . . . , e[b][n][ρ]_ ) in R _[n]_ .
comments.explanation: Let me verify: φ(ρ) = Σ a_i e^{b_i ρ} = 0 means the dot product a · ϱ(ρ) = 0, which is exactly orthogonality. This is correct. The set S_ρ of coefficient vectors a for which ρ is a root is indeed the hyperplane orthogonal to ϱ(ρ), hence an (n−1)-dimensional linear subspace. No issue here.

comments.title: Volume ratio in proof sketch is ill-defined
comments.quote: The desired probability is therefore upper bounded by max _ρ_ VOL( _∪Sy | y ∈_ [ _ρ − ϵ, ρ_ + _ϵ_ ]) _/_ VOL( _Sy | y ∈_ R)
comments.explanation: This is a proof sketch, but the expression VOL(S_y | y ∈ ℝ) is the volume of the union of hyperplanes over all y ∈ ℝ, which in ℝ^n has infinite Lebesgue measure — so the ratio as written is not literally meaningful. In context, the intended meaning is a probability bound involving the density κ times the measure of the ϵ-tube around the union of hyperplanes restricted to the bounded region {|a_i| ≤ R}. A careful reader who understands the setup can reconstruct the intended argument, but the literal expression is not a well-defined ratio. Since this is an informal proof sketch with full proofs in Appendix C.3, this is minor.

comments.title: Theorem 4 concentration bound statement appears trivially true
comments.explanation: The inequality E[sup_s D(T,s)] ≤ sup_s E[D(T,s)] + O(√(T log(TK))) is the substantive content: it bounds the expected supremum by the supremum of expectations plus a concentration term. This is standard in VC-type arguments where the additive term comes from a union bound over a finite 'effective' set of axis-aligned paths (bounded via shattering). The O(√(T log(TK))) term depends on K and T but not explicitly on the shattering constant k from Theorem 3 or on p — this is likely because those are treated as constants and absorbed in the O(·). This is a reasonable presentation for a theorem statement whose full proof is deferred, so not an issue.

comments.title: Polynomial kernel formula for multi-metric combination has stray exponent
comments.explanation: Reading Theorem 8, I expected the polynomial kernel for combined metrics to be $w(u,v) = (\sum_{i=1}^{p-1} \rho_i \tilde{d}_i(u,v) + \rho_p)^d$, i.e., a weighted sum of $p-1$ similarity functions plus an offset $\rho_p$, raised to the degree $d$. The OCR output has the '-1' separated from 'p' and there's only one $\tilde{d}(u,v)$ (not indexed by $i$). The most plausible intended formula uses $\tilde{d}_i(u,v)$ — one similarity function per metric — since the whole point of Section 4.1.2 is combining several similarity measures. As written (with a single $\tilde{d}(u,v)$ not depending on $i$), the sum collapses to $(\sum \rho_i) \tilde{d}(u,v) + \rho_p$, which would defeat the purpose of having multiple metrics. This is likely an OCR/typesetting artifact rather than an author error, but since the formula is the central object of Theorem 8 and the reader needs to know whether each metric has its own $\tilde{d}_i$, the ambiguity is worth flagging.

comments.title: Proof sketch of Theorem 7 has garbled sentence about polynomial equation
comments.quote: The key technical challenge is to show that for any _α_ ˜ with degree at most _nd_ , and coefficients that are jointly _u ∈ U Kκ_ , _f_ ( _u_ -bounded, where) = 1 _/_ 2 is a polynomial equation in _K_ is a constant that only depends on
comments.explanation: This sentence is severely garbled by the OCR. Reconstructing the intended meaning: 'for any $u \in U$, $f(u)=1/2$ is a polynomial equation in $\tilde{\alpha}$ with degree at most $nd$, and coefficients that are jointly $K\kappa$-bounded, where $K$ is a constant that only depends on $d$ and the support of $\tilde{d}(u,v)$.' Given that this is plausibly just OCR rearrangement of a correct sentence, I would normally not flag it. However, the key quantitative claim — that coefficients are jointly bounded (by some function of $\kappa$) — is the crux of invoking Balcan et al. [2020b]'s dispersion machinery, and a reader cannot verify the constant $K$ depends only on $d$ and the support without this sentence being legible. Flagging as minor since it's likely an OCR issue rather than an author issue, but worth noting the reader cannot check the key bound from the sketch as rendered.

comments.title: Semi-bandit loss estimator appears to have inconsistent integration variable
comments.explanation: I tried to parse this as the standard importance-weighted semi-bandit estimator. Normally one would write $\hat{l}_t(\rho) = \mathbb{I}[\rho \in A^{(t)}(\rho_t)] \cdot l_t(\rho) / \int_{A^{(t)}(\rho_t)} p_t(\rho')\, d\rho'$, so that $\mathbb{E}[\hat{l}_t(\rho)] = l_t(\rho)$. As written, the indicator is $\mathbb{I}[\rho \in A^{(t)}(\rho)]$ (should be $\rho_t$ or the sampled point), and the denominator appears to be $\int_{A^{(t)}(\rho)} p_t(\rho) l_t(\rho) d\rho$ which mixes the argument $\rho$ with the integration variable and bizarrely includes $l_t(\rho)$ inside the normalizer. Some of this is likely OCR mangling (the formula display is corrupted), but the expression as printed is not a well-defined unbiased estimator. A careful reader trying to reproduce Algorithm 2 would not be able to determine the correct normalization from this passage. Even allowing for OCR, the indicator's argument and the integrand of the denominator are both suspicious enough that the intended meaning is not unambiguous.

comments.title: Theorem 9 statement mismatched with scope (claims 'each objective in Table 1')
comments.quote: _For the each objective in Table 1 and exponential kernel (Definition 1c), there exists an algorithm which outputs the interval containing σ in time O_[˜] ( _n_[4] ) _._
comments.explanation: The text preceding Theorem 9 describes feedback-set algorithms explicitly for the mincut objective (Algorithm 3) and the harmonic objective (Algorithm 4). The theorem then states the result holds for 'each objective in Table 1', which includes normalized cut and label propagation. Nothing in the passage justifies the extension to these other two objectives, and Theorem 7 earlier in the paper specifically notes that the polynomial-kernel result 'extends to all Table 1 objectives except mincut', hinting that objective-by-objective differences matter. This may be justified in the appendix, but as a reader of this passage I cannot verify the claim covers normalized cut / label propagation. Given the summary says the previous sentence only mentions mincut and harmonic, the theorem statement risks overclaiming relative to what is demonstrated in the main text.

comments.title: Pseudodimension upper bound argument loses a factor
comments.quote: The pseudo-dimension _m_ satisfies 2 _[m] ≤ O_ ( _mn_[2] ) and is therefore _O_ (log _n_ ).
comments.explanation: The argument says the real line is partitioned into $O(mn^2)$ intervals, giving at most that many distinct binary labelings of the $m$-sample. For pseudodimension we need $2^m \leq$ number of achievable labelings. So the inequality $2^m \leq O(mn^2)$ is correct, and solving gives $m = O(\log m + \log n) = O(\log n)$ as claimed. Checked carefully — this does go through since $\log m$ is dominated once $m$ grows large. So the claim is fine; I initially worried the $m$ factor was being dropped but $2^m = \Omega(m n^2)$ forces $m = O(\log n)$ since otherwise $2^m$ would grow super-polynomially in $n$ while the RHS is polynomial. No issue.

comments.title: Theorem 12 statement is garbled / unreadable
comments.quote: R_ ˆ( _F, S_ ) _≤ O_ (min _{_ ~~~~ _u_ ( _xd/Ti_ ( _ρ_ )) log = _f RTρ_ ( _xi_ +) _for LT i[−] ∈[β] ,_ [ _T_ ~~~~ ] _are piecewise L-Lipschitz andPdim_ ( _F_ ) _/T }_ ) _. β-dispersed. Then_
comments.explanation: Even accounting for OCR issues, the statement of Theorem 12 is severely mangled — the hypothesis ('the functions u(x_i)(ρ) = f_ρ(x_i) + ... are piecewise L-Lipschitz and β-dispersed') is mixed with the conclusion (a bound on empirical Rademacher complexity R̂(F,S)). The intended statement appears to be something like R̂(F,S) ≤ O(min{√(d/T log(RT) + L T^{-β}), √(Pdim(F)/T)}), but as rendered, a reader cannot reliably reconstruct the precise bound — particularly the dependence on R, L, β, and d. Since this theorem is the technical foundation for the subsequent generalization claim (Theorem 13), the garbled rendering obscures whether the stated Theorem 13 bound is actually what follows. This is likely an OCR failure rather than an author error, but it affects readability of a key formal result in the passage being checked.

comments.title: Theorem 13 bound uses d without specifying which d
comments.explanation: The generalization bound is stated as O(√(d log T log(1/δ) / T)) where d is the dimension of the parameter space C ⊂ R^d from Theorem 12. However, the paper uses d in at least two other senses: (i) the polynomial degree in the polynomial/kernel family (Definition 1b) and (ii) the similarity metric d(·,·). Elsewhere in the paper the parameter-space dimension is denoted p (e.g., Theorem 3, Theorem 4: 'R^p'). Reusing d here for the parameter dimension — especially in a theorem whose application includes the single-parameter σ case (d=1) and the multi-metric case — risks confusion, particularly because the unweighted case has pseudodimension Θ(log n), which should appear in the bound when Pdim dominates. The reader has to infer this from context rather than seeing it stated. Minor, but the instantiation of the bound for the two cases discussed (weighted via dispersion, unweighted via pseudodimension) is left implicit, which for a main generalization theorem is a modest gap in exposition.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and underexplored issue in graph-based semi-supervised learning—how to choose or learn the graph construction rather than treating it as a heuristic—and the proposed connection to data-driven algorithm design appears potentially significant, especially if the claimed online regret and distributional generalization guarantees are technically strong. The introduction conveys a clear high-level motivation and situates the work well within graph-based SSL, but the presentation is currently somewhat difficult to follow: the problem formulation and Table 1 contain formatting/notation issues that obscure key definitions, and several central concepts—what constitutes an “instance,” what supervision is available across training instances, what loss is optimized, and which graph families/hyperparameters are covered—should be stated more concretely before the technical contributions. The claimed contributions are ambitious, but the novelty relative to prior hyperparameter learning, metric learning, graph construction, and data-driven algorithm design work needs sharper delineation; in particular, the reader needs a clearer explanation of why existing validation/model-selection approaches are insufficient and what assumptions make the new guarantees meaningful in SSL practice. The most significant concern is that the paper’s practical relevance depends heavily on whether the learned graph parameters transfer across realistic domains and whether the algorithms scale, so the paper should ensure that the formal setting, assumptions, and eventual empirical or implementation evidence convincingly support this. Overall, the work appears theoretically strong and promising, but its clarity and impact would benefit from a cleaner formulation, more explicit assumptions, and a more precise statement of contributions and limitations.

comments.title: Graph parameter ρ is described as an SSL algorithm rather than a graph-construction hyperparameter
comments.quote: Semi-supervised learning. We examine commonly used parameterized graph families, denoted by general notation G(ρ), where ρ corresponds to a semi-supervised learning algorithm.
comments.explanation: The later setup appears to fix a graph-labeling algorithm A(G,L,U), while ρ parameterizes the graph family G(ρ), such as a threshold, polynomial offset, or RBF bandwidth. Describing ρ as corresponding to a semi-supervised learning algorithm blurs the distinction between the graph-construction hyperparameter and the labeling algorithm, which matters because later claims compare graph families and optimization algorithms separately.

comments.title: Per-instance prediction loss appears unnormalized despite later use as a bounded loss
comments.quote: for a loss function l : Y × Y → [0,1] and a target labeling τ : U → Y, we need to find argmin ρ∈P lA(G(ρ),L,U) := ∑U l(A(G(ρ),L,U)(u), τ(u)).
comments.explanation: As written, l_A is a sum of losses in [0,1], so it lies in [0,|U|], not [0,1]. For one fixed instance, summing or averaging gives the same argmin, but later Algorithm 1 explicitly averages by |U_t|^{-1}, which is important for bounded-loss regret analyses and for comparing instances with different numbers of unlabeled points. The definition should either include a factor 1/|U| or later bounded-loss claims should account for the scaling.

comments.title: Polynomial graph kernel may produce inadmissible negative edge weights unless the parameter domain is constrained
comments.quote: Polynomial kernel, G(α̃). w(u,v) = (d̃(u,v) + α̃)^d for fixed degree d, parameterized by α̃.
comments.explanation: Graph-based SSL objectives in the paper use weighted graphs, Laplacians, cuts, and smoothness penalties, which conventionally require nonnegative edge weights. If d̃(u,v) is a dot product, then d̃(u,v)+α̃ can be negative unless the data or α̃ are constrained. For odd d this directly gives negative weights; for even d the intended admissible range is still unclear. The definition should specify conditions such as d̃(u,v)+α̃ ≥ 0 for all relevant pairs, or explain how negative kernel values are handled as graph weights.

comments.title: Proof sketch for Theorem 2 uses an ill-defined volume ratio over lower-dimensional subspaces
comments.quote: The desired probability is therefore upper bounded by max ρ VOL(∪Sy | y ∈ [ρ − ϵ, ρ + ϵ]) / VOL(Sy | y ∈ R) which we will show to be Õ(ϵ).
comments.explanation: The intended anti-concentration argument is plausible, but the displayed ratio is not well defined as written. Each S_y is a codimension-one subspace and has zero n-dimensional Lebesgue measure unless a thickening or bounded ambient intersection is specified. The denominator is also unclear, especially because the coefficient distribution is assumed to have bounded density rather than to be uniform. The proof sketch should instead explicitly bound the n-dimensional volume of coefficients in the bounded support for which there exists y in a short interval with φ(y)=0, and then multiply by the density bound.

comments.title: Theorem 4 alone does not imply dispersion without a small-crossing-probability condition
comments.quote: We employ tools from algebraic geometry to establish a bound on shattering of algebraic hypersurfaces by axis-aligned paths (Theorem 3), which implies dispersion using a VC dimension based argument (Theorem 4).
comments.explanation: Theorem 4 appears to give a concentration-style inequality of the form E[sup_s D(T,s)] ≤ sup_s E[D(T,s)] plus a complexity term. That does not by itself ensure that short paths have expected crossing count O(ϵT), which is the distributional condition needed for dispersion. The prose should say that Theorems 3–4 reduce dispersion to bounding the expected number of discontinuity hypersurfaces crossed by short paths, rather than implying dispersion by themselves.

comments.title: Theorem 5 overloads U as both the unlabeled set and a set of labelings
comments.quote: There exists a data instance (L,U) such that ... there exists a set of labelings U of the unlabeled points such that for some Uζ, Ūζ ∈ U, rζ minimizes lA(G(r),L,Uζ) but not lA(G(r),L,Ūζ).
comments.explanation: Earlier U denotes the unlabeled sample, while target labels are represented separately by τ:U→Y. In Theorem 5, U is also used for a set of labelings, and Uζ is passed as the third argument to l_A, where the third argument should be the unlabeled set rather than a labeling. The intended construction is likely to keep the point set fixed and vary the target labeling, but the notation makes it unclear whether Uζ is a labeling, a relabeled dataset, or an unlabeled node set. A clearer statement would write something like l_A(G(r),L,U;τζ).

comments.title: Regret for the randomized online algorithm is defined without clarifying expectation or realization
comments.quote: At time t ∈ [T] we predict ρt ∈ P ... and seek to minimize regret RT := ∑t lA(G(ρt),Lt,Ut) − minρ∈P ∑t lA(G(ρ),Lt,Ut).
comments.explanation: The regret expression is the standard fixed-comparator regret, but Algorithm 1 samples ρ from a distribution. The definition does not state whether R_T is the realized random regret, expected regret over the learner's randomness, or a high-probability quantity. Since exponential-weights analyses for dispersed losses usually state expected or high-probability regret bounds, the objective should explicitly distinguish realized and expected regret.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 does not specify the compact parameter domain used for sampling
comments.quote: Set w1(ρ) = 1 for all ρ ∈ R≥0. ... Sample ρ with probability pt(ρ) = wt(ρ)/Wt, where Wt := ∫C wt(ρ)dρ.
comments.explanation: Continuous exponential-weights algorithms normally operate over a bounded parameter set C or P. Algorithm 1 initializes weights on all of R≥0 but normalizes only over C, and C is not listed as an input or explicitly identified with the feasible parameter domain. If C were literally R≥0, W_1 would be infinite; if C is bounded, initialization and sampling should be restricted to it. This affects both implementability and the stated regret guarantees, which depend on finite domain volume.

comments.title: Theorem 7's anti-concentration assumption is weaker than the proof appears to require
comments.quote: If d̃(u,v) follows a κ-bounded distribution with a closed and bounded support, the sequence is 1/2-dispersed, and the regret of Algorithm 1 may be upper bounded by Õ(√T).
comments.explanation: The preceding prose refers to distances being jointly κ-bounded, but the theorem statement reads as a marginal condition on each d̃(u,v). Marginal bounded densities are generally insufficient: distances within one instance could be strongly correlated so that a discontinuity location is deterministic or highly concentrated even though every individual distance has bounded density. The proof sketch also relies on polynomial coefficients being jointly bounded-density, but bounded density of the original distances is not automatically preserved under nonlinear maps to determinant coefficients. The theorem should state the stronger joint or induced-coefficient anti-concentration condition actually needed.

comments.title: Closed-form harmonic solution requires an unstated nonsingularity or connectivity condition
comments.quote: The solution of the quadratic objective is given by fU = (DUU − WUU)^{-1} WUL fL.
comments.explanation: This standard harmonic-function formula is valid only when D_UU − W_UU is invertible, typically requiring every connected component containing an unlabeled node to also contain at least one labeled node, or requiring additional regularization. The theorem statement does not explicitly impose such a condition or specify a pseudoinverse or tie-breaking convention. If singular cases are possible, the loss may be undefined, non-unique, or have discontinuities not captured solely by roots of f(u)=1/2.

comments.title: Formulas quoted for Zhou et al. classification appear to miss matrix inverses
comments.quote: the labels are given by a closed-form matrix, f* = (I − αD^{-1/2}WD^{1/2})Y or f* = (D − αW)Y
comments.explanation: For the local/global consistency methods of Zhou et al., the standard solutions involve solving a linear system, for example terms like (I−αS)^{-1}Y or (D−αW)^{-1}Y, often up to scalar factors. The displayed formulas multiply by the matrices directly instead of by their inverses. If this is an OCR or typographical omission, it should be corrected; if it is printed this way, it changes the algorithm and the subsequent reasoning about where discontinuities arise.

comments.title: The final uniform-convergence theorem omits key assumptions and dependencies needed by the preceding bounds
comments.quote: For both weighted and unweighted graph w(u,v) defined above, with probability at least 1−δ, the average loss ... with respect to any parameter ρ ∈ R^d satisfies ...
comments.explanation: Theorem 13 appears to give a broad high-probability uniform-convergence guarantee for all weighted and unweighted graph constructions and all parameters in R^d. The preceding machinery, however, requires additional hypotheses: a bounded parameter domain C lying in a ball of radius R, piecewise Lipschitz losses, a dispersion rate or pseudodimension bound, and distributional anti-concentration assumptions. The displayed rate also suppresses dependencies that should come from Theorem 12, such as R, the Lipschitz constant, the dispersion rate β, or the relevant pseudodimension; it is therefore unclear how the bound reduces to a function only of d, T, and δ. Finally, earlier dispersion statements are phrased in expectation, while Theorem 13 states a high-probability guarantee over the sample, so the conversion from expected dispersion/Rademacher bounds to the stated probability claim needs to be justified. As written, the theorem reads as an unconditional generalization result that is not supported by the stated assumptions.

comments.title: Graph parameter \(\rho\) is described as an SSL algorithm rather than a graph/hyperparameter
comments.quote: _Semi-supervised learning._ We examine commonly used parameterized graph families, denoted by general notation _G_ ( _ρ_ ), where _ρ_ corresponds to a semi-supervised learning algorithm.
comments.explanation: At first I read this as saying that each value of \(\rho\) selects a different semi-supervised labeling algorithm. The subsequent setup resolves part of the intent: the paper fixes a graph labeling algorithm \(A(G,L,U)\), while \(\rho\in P\) parameterizes the graph family \(G(\rho)\) — e.g., a threshold, polynomial offset, or RBF bandwidth. Thus the intended object being learned is a graph-construction hyperparameter, not the SSL algorithm itself. This is a localized wording issue, but it could mislead readers because later claims distinguish combinations of graph families and optimization algorithms; saying \(\rho\) “corresponds to” the SSL algorithm blurs that distinction.

comments.title: Per-instance prediction loss appears unnormalized despite later use as a bounded loss
comments.quote: More formally, for a loss function _l_ : _Y × Y →_ [0 _,_ 1] and a target labeling _τ_ : _U →Y_ , we need to find argmin _ρ∈P lA_ ( _G_ ( _ρ_ ) _,L,U_ ) :=[] _U[l]_[(] _[A][G]_[(] _[ρ]_[)] _[,L,U]_[(] _[u]_[)] _[, τ]_[(] _[u]_[))][.]
comments.explanation: The intent seems clear: evaluate the labels predicted by \(A\) on the unlabeled set against the target labeling \(\tau\). For a single fixed instance, minimizing the sum or the average over \(U\) gives the same \(\arg\min\), so the omission would not affect this displayed optimization problem. However, as written this defines \(l_A\) as a sum of losses in \([0,1]\), hence it lies in \([0,|U|]\), not \([0,1]\). Later in Algorithm 1 the loss is explicitly averaged by \(|U_t|^{-1}\), which is important for multiplicative-weights style regret bounds and for comparing instances with different numbers of unlabeled nodes. If the intended definition here is the average loss, a normalization factor \(1/|U|\) should be included; if the intended definition is the sum, later bounded-loss claims need to account for the scaling.

comments.title: Labeled examples are pairs but are also treated as graph nodes/data points
comments.quote: We are given some unlabeled points _U ⊂X_ and labeled points _L ⊂X × Y_ , such that _|L|_ + _|U |_ = _n_ . One constructs a graph _G_ by placing (possibly weighted) edges _w_ ( _u, v_ ) between pairs of data points _u, v_ which are ‘similar’
comments.explanation: I understand the standard abuse of notation here: a labeled example is a pair \((x,y)\), but the graph node is the feature point \(x\), with \(y\) stored as its observed label. Still, the text alternates between \(L\subset X\times Y\) and treating elements of \(L\cup U\) as data points on which distances and edge weights are computed. Without explicitly saying that the graph uses the projection of \(L\) onto \(X\), expressions such as distances between nodes in \(L\cup U\) are formally ill-typed. This is easy to fix by defining \(L_X=\{x:(x,y)\in L\}\) or by stating that the authors abuse notation and write \(L\) for the labeled feature points when constructing the graph.

comments.title: Polynomial graph kernel may produce negative edge weights unless the parameter domain is constrained
comments.quote: _b) Polynomial kernel, G_ (˜ _α_ ) _. w_ ( _u, v_ ) = ( _d_[˜] ( _u, v_ ) + _α_ ˜) _[d] for fixed degree d, parameterized by α_ ˜ _.
comments.explanation: The definition is recognizable as the usual polynomial kernel form, and the footnote later clarifies that \(\tilde d(u,v)\) may be a dot product. The issue is that graph-based SSL objectives in the paper use weighted graphs, Laplacians, cuts, and smoothness penalties, all of which conventionally require nonnegative edge weights. If \(\tilde d(u,v)=\langle u,v\rangle\), then \(\tilde d(u,v)+\tilde\alpha\) can be negative unless the data are normalized/nonnegative or \(\tilde\alpha\) is restricted. For odd degree this directly gives negative weights; even degree avoids negativity but still leaves the intended admissible parameter range unclear. The definition should specify conditions such as \(\tilde d(u,v)+\tilde\alpha\ge 0\) for all pairs under consideration, or otherwise explain how negative kernel values are handled as graph weights.

comments.title: The term “similarity function” is used for quantities with opposite monotonic directions
comments.quote: We assume there is a feature based _similarity function d_ : _X × X →_ R _≥_ 0, a metric which monotonically captures pairwise similarity.
comments.explanation: The surrounding definitions mostly make the intended convention recoverable: for threshold graphs and Gaussian RBF graphs, \(d(u,v)\) behaves like a distance, since smaller values mean more similar examples; later the authors explicitly say that \(\tilde d\) in the polynomial kernel increases with similarity “as opposed to the other two.” Thus this is not a mathematical flaw in the constructions. Still, the quoted sentence calls \(d\) a “similarity function” and “a metric,” which in standard usage point in opposite directions: metrics increase with dissimilarity, while similarity scores usually increase with similarity. Since the paper uses both \(d\) and \(\tilde d\) with opposite monotonicity conventions, this sentence would be clearer if it stated that \(d\) is a distance/dissimilarity metric, while \(\tilde d\) is a similarity score.

comments.title: Proof sketch for Theorem 2 uses a problematic volume ratio over lower-dimensional subspaces
comments.quote: The desired probability is therefore upper bounded by max _ρ_ VOL( _∪Sy | y ∈_ [ _ρ − ϵ, ρ_ + _ϵ_ ]) _/_ VOL( _Sy | y ∈_ R) which we will show to be _O_[˜] ( _ϵ_ ).
comments.explanation: The intended argument is clear: for each possible root location y, the coefficients a satisfying φ(y)=0 lie on a codimension-one linear subspace, and the union of these subspaces over a short interval should occupy only O(ϵ) volume inside the bounded coefficient domain. That is a plausible route to the stated probability bound under a bounded density assumption. However, as written, the displayed volume ratio is not well defined or justified. Each S_y is an (n−1)-dimensional subspace of R^n and has n-dimensional Lebesgue measure zero unless one takes a thickening or intersects the union over y with the bounded support box. The denominator “VOL(S_y | y∈R)” is also unclear: if it means the union over all y, it is not the ambient coefficient volume and need not be the right normalizing quantity, especially since the coefficient distribution is only assumed to have bounded density, not to be uniform. The finite coefficient range mentioned earlier likely resolves the intended proof, but the sketch should explicitly bound the n-dimensional volume of {a∈[-R,R]^n : ∃y∈I, a·ϱ(y)=0} and multiply by κ, rather than using this ratio.

comments.title: Theorem 5 overloads U as both the unlabeled set and a set of labelings
comments.quote: There exists a data instance_ ( _L, U_ ) _such that for any rζ_ = _ζr_ min + (1 _− ζ_ ) _r_ max _for ζ ∈_ (0 _,_ 1) _, there exists a set of labelings U of the unlabeled points such that for some Uζ, U_[¯] _ζ ∈U, rζ minimizes lA_ ( _G_ ( _r_ ) _,L,Uζ_ ) _but not lA_ ( _G_ ( _r_ ) _,L,_ ¯ _Uζ_ ) _.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended construction: fix the same point set and vary the true labeling of the unlabeled nodes to show that different thresholds can be optimal or suboptimal. However, the notation conflicts with the setup. Earlier U denotes the set of unlabeled examples, while the target labels are represented separately by τ:U→Y and l_A(G(ρ),L,U) is defined using U as the unlabeled sample, with τ implicit in the loss. Here the theorem says there is a “set of labelings U” and then passes Uζ as the third argument to l_A, where the third argument should be the unlabeled set, not a labeling. This is likely intended to be a calligraphic set of labelings or a family of target functions, but as written it obscures whether Uζ is a labeling, a relabeled dataset, or an unlabeled node set. A clearer statement would keep the unlabeled set fixed and write something like l_A(G(r),L,U;τ_ζ).

comments.title: Theorem 4 alone does not imply dispersion without a small-crossing-probability condition
comments.quote: We employ tools from algebraic geometry to establish a bound on shattering of algebraic hypersurfaces by axis-aligned paths (Theorem 3), which implies dispersion using a VC dimension based argument (Theorem 4).
comments.explanation: The direction of the argument is plausible: a finite VC dimension for path–hypersurface intersections can give a uniform convergence bound, and if each short path has small expected intersection count, this yields dispersion. The stated Theorem 4, however, only gives a concentration-style inequality of the form E[sup_s D(T,s)] ≤ sup_s E[D(T,s)] plus a complexity term. It does not by itself ensure that sup_s E[D(T,s)] is O(ϵT) for short paths, which is the substantive distributional condition needed for dispersion. This may be supplied later for the graph families, and the theorem is useful as a tool, but the prose slightly overstates what Theorem 4 alone establishes. It would be more precise to say that Theorems 3–4 reduce dispersion to bounding the expected number of discontinuity hypersurfaces crossed by short axis-aligned paths.

comments.title: Regret for the randomized online algorithm is defined without clarifying expectation or realization
comments.quote: At time _t ∈_ [ _T_ ] we predict _ρt ∈P_ (the parameter space) ... and seek _T_ to minimize regret _RT_ :=[] _[T] t_ =1 _[l][A]_[(] _[G]_[(] _[ρ] t_[)] _[,L] t[,U] t_[)] _[ −]_[min] _[ρ][∈P]_  _t_ =1 _[l][A]_[(] _[G]_[(] _[ρ]_[)] _[,L] t[,U] t_[)][.]
comments.explanation: The regret expression is the standard fixed-comparator regret, and the surrounding discussion of full information is consistent with online learning. The potential ambiguity arises because the next algorithm samples ρ from a distribution, while this definition does not say whether R_T is the realized random regret, expected regret over the learner’s randomness, or a high-probability quantity. Exponential-weights analyses for dispersed losses usually state expected or high-probability regret bounds. This is probably clarified later, but at the point where the objective is introduced, a careful reader could misread the guarantee as deterministic regret for the sampled sequence. Adding “in expectation over the learner’s randomization” or explicitly distinguishing realized and expected regret would remove the ambiguity.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 does not specify the compact parameter domain used for sampling
comments.quote:
Set _w_ 1( _ρ_ ) = 1 for all _ρ ∈_ R _≥_ 0.

- 6: Sample _ρ_ with probability _pt_ ( _ρ_ ) = _[w] W[t]_[(] _t[ρ]_[)][, output as] _[ ρ][t]_[, where] _[ W][t]_[:=]  _C[w][t]_[(] _[ρ]_[)] _[dρ]_[.]
comments.explanation: The intended algorithm is clearly the continuous exponential-weights scheme from the dispersion literature, and such algorithms normally operate over a bounded parameter set C or P. I checked the surrounding context: the paper has a parameter space P and the dispersion definition refers to a set C, so the authors likely intend C to be the feasible hyperparameter domain. However, Algorithm 1 initializes weights on all of R_{≥0} while normalizing only over C, and C is not listed as an input or explicitly identified with P. If C were literally R_{≥0}, W_t is infinite at t=1; if C is a bounded interval, the initialization and the sampling distribution should be restricted to it. This is not just a typographical concern, because the regret guarantees and implementability of continuous exponential weights require a finite-volume domain and sometimes depend logarithmically on its size or covering scale.

comments.title: Theorem 7’s anti-concentration assumption is weaker in the formal statement than in the preceding prose
comments.quote: **Theorem 7.** _Let l_ 1 _, . . . , lT_ : R _→_ R _denote an independent sequence of losses as a function of α_ ˜ _, for graph with edges w_ ( _u, v_ ) = ( _d_[˜] ( _u, v_ ) + _α_ ˜) _[d] labeled by optimizing the quadratic objective_  _u,v[w]_[(] _[u, v]_[)(] _[f]_[(] _[u]_[)] _[ −][f]_[(] _[v]_[))][2] _[.][If][d]_[˜][(] _[u, v]_[)] _[ follows a][ κ][-bounded distribution with a closed and bounded] support, the sequence is_[1] 2 _[-dispersed, and the regret of Algorithm 1 may be upper bounded by][O]_[˜][(] _√T_ ) _._
comments.explanation: The paragraph before the theorem says the distances are assumed to be jointly κ-bounded on a closed bounded support, which is a plausible kind of anti-concentration assumption for proving dispersion. But the theorem itself only says each \tilde d(u,v) follows a κ-bounded distribution, which reads as a marginal condition. A marginal bounded-density condition is generally not enough for the polynomial-root anti-concentration needed here: distances within one instance could be strongly correlated so that a discontinuity location is deterministic or highly concentrated even though every individual distance has a bounded density. The proof sketch also says the relevant polynomial coefficients are jointly Kκ-bounded, but bounded density of the original distances is not automatically preserved under nonlinear maps to determinant coefficients; products and other polynomial transformations can create unbounded densities or singular pushforwards. This may well be handled in Appendix C.2, but as stated in the passage the formal theorem appears to omit the stronger joint/induced-coefficient anti-concentration condition that the argument needs.

comments.title: Closed-form harmonic solution requires an unstated nonsingularity/connectivity condition
comments.quote: _Proof Sketch._ The solution of the quadratic objective is given by _fU_ = ( _DUU − WUU_ ) _[−]_[1] _WULfL_ .
comments.explanation: This is the standard harmonic-function formula obtained from the unlabeled block of the graph Laplacian, so the form itself is recognizable. However, it is only valid when D_{UU}-W_{UU} is invertible, equivalently under the usual condition that every connected component containing an unlabeled node also contains at least one labeled node, or under some additional regularization that makes the system nonsingular. I checked whether the theorem statement supplies such a condition: it assumes bounded random similarities but does not explicitly restrict the polynomial-kernel parameter range to guarantee strictly positive connecting weights, nor does it specify a tie-breaking or pseudoinverse convention for singular cases. If singularities are possible, the loss can be undefined, non-unique, or acquire additional discontinuities not captured merely by the roots of f(u)=1/2. This is a localized fix, but the proof sketch relies directly on the inverse.

comments.title: Formulas quoted for Zhou et al. classification appear to miss matrix inverses
comments.quote: _The key observation is that the labels are given by a closed-form matrix, f[∗]_ = ( _I − αD[−]_[1] _[/]_[2] _WD_[1] _[/]_[2] ) _Y or f[∗]_ = ( _D − αW_ ) _Y (for the two variants considered)._
comments.explanation: The intended reference seems to be the local/global consistency methods of Zhou et al. [2004]. In the standard formulas, the solution involves solving a linear system, e.g. a matrix inverse such as (I-αS)^{-1}Y with S=D^{-1/2}WD^{-1/2}, and related variants involving (D-αW)^{-1}Y, often up to a scalar factor. The displayed expressions in the passage instead multiply by (I-αD^{-1/2}WD^{1/2}) or (D-αW) directly. I considered whether this is just an OCR omission, especially since inverse superscripts are easy to lose in extraction; if so, it should simply be corrected. But if the formula is as printed, it is mathematically wrong and also changes the subsequent claim about where discontinuities arise: with the inverse, denominators/determinants matter; without it, the dependence is only polynomial and describes a different algorithm.

comments.title: The final uniform-convergence claim is stated more broadly than the stated assumptions support
comments.quote: **Theorem 13.** _For both weighted and unweighted graph w_ ( _u, v_ ) _defined above, with probability at least_ 1 _− δ, the average loss on any sample x_ 1 _, . . . , xT ∼ D[T] , the loss suffered w.r.t. to any T d_ log _T_ log 1 _/δ parameter ρ ∈_ R _[d] satisfies | T_[1]  _i_ =1 _[l][ρ]_[(] _[x][i]_[)] _[ −]_[E] _[x][∼][D][l][ρ]_[(] _[x]_[)] _[| ≤][O] T ._
comments.explanation: I understand the intended conclusion: combine the empirical Rademacher bound from Balcan et al. with the paper’s dispersion or pseudodimension results to get a distributional ERM guarantee. However, the theorem as stated appears to apply to all weighted and unweighted graph constructions “defined above” and to all parameters in \(\mathbb R^d\), without restating the conditions under which the preceding machinery applies. The dispersion results earlier depended on structural/randomness assumptions such as bounded-density perturbations or random coefficients, piecewise Lipschitz behavior, bounded parameter domains, and a specific dispersion rate. Theorem 12 also explicitly assumes \(C\) lies in a ball of radius \(R\) and that the induced functions on the sample are piecewise \(L\)-Lipschitz and \(\beta\)-dispersed. None of these hypotheses appears in Theorem 13. A reader could therefore take Theorem 13 as an unconditional uniform-convergence result for every graph family and every instance distribution, which does not follow from the cited theorem or from the earlier dispersion arguments. This is more than a cosmetic omission because the guarantee can fail without control of discontinuity concentration or parameter-domain size.

comments.title: Parameter domain in Theorem 13 conflicts with the bounded-domain requirement used to prove it
comments.quote: _Let F_ = _{fρ_ : _X →_ [0 _,_ 1] _, ρ ∈C ⊂_ R _[d] } be a parametereized family of functions, where C lies in a ball of radius R._
comments.explanation: The quoted assumption in Theorem 12 is standard for these dispersion/Rademacher bounds: the parameter set must be bounded, with the radius entering the covering or chaining argument. But Theorem 13 later states the guarantee for “any parameter \(\rho \in \mathbb R^d\).” Earlier graph parameters such as \(r\ge 0\) and \(\sigma\ge 0\) are naturally unbounded unless explicitly truncated, and \(\sigma=0\) is also a boundary value needing interpretation for the exponential kernel. Perhaps the intended domain is a compact interval such as the online algorithm’s \(C\), or an effective interval like \([r_{\min},r_{\max}]\) for threshold graphs, but that restriction is not stated here. Without such a restriction, the application of Theorem 12 is not justified and the bound cannot be interpreted with its dependence on \(R\).

comments.title: The displayed rate in Theorem 13 suppresses dependencies needed by the preceding bounds
comments.quote: _For the unweighted case, we can use the pseudodimension bounds above, and for the weighted case we use dispersion guarantees from section 4.1. For either case it suffices to bound the empirical Rademacher complexity._
comments.explanation: The strategy is reasonable, but the stated conclusion seems to skip over which branch of Theorem 12 is being used and what dependencies remain. Theorem 12’s dispersion-based term should depend on quantities such as the parameter dimension, the bounded-domain radius \(R\), the Lipschitz constant \(L\), and the dispersion rate \(\beta\) through a term of the form roughly \(\sqrt{d\log(RT)/T}+L T^{-\beta}\), while the pseudodimension branch gives a rate involving \(\operatorname{Pdim}(F)\). In contrast, Theorem 13 appears to state a single rate depending only on \(d\), \(T\), and \(\delta\). If the unweighted case is handled by pseudodimension, the theorem should say what the relevant pseudodimension bound is and how it reduces to the displayed expression; if the weighted case is handled by dispersion, the theorem should either include or explicitly justify suppressing \(R,L,\beta\). This matters especially because Theorem 11 has just stated \(\operatorname{Pdim}(H_\sigma)=\Theta(n)\), so a reader may be confused about why no dependence on \(n\) or on the pseudodimension appears in the final rate.

comments.title: High-probability uniform convergence from expected dispersion/Rademacher bounds is not justified in the text
comments.quote: _Now, using classic results from learning theory, we conclude that ERM has good generalization._
comments.explanation: The overall route through empirical Rademacher complexity is standard, and the cited theorem is plausibly the right tool. What remains unclear is the probabilistic conversion. Earlier dispersion statements in the paper are phrased in expectation over random instances or random coefficients, whereas Theorem 13 gives a high-probability statement over \(x_1,\ldots,x_T\sim D^T\). To get this, one normally needs either a high-probability dispersion/Rademacher bound or an argument converting the expected bound to high probability, followed by the usual Rademacher generalization inequality. The passage jumps directly from the empirical-complexity theorem to a high-probability uniform-convergence bound without explaining whether the sample-dependent functions satisfy dispersion with high probability, in expectation only, or deterministically under the assumptions. This is fixable, but as written the final probability statement is under-justified.
